coredata.dc:title: Water dispersible green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for high sensitive detection of curcumin and cell imaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Qin, Xue-Zhen
Pan, Cong-Jie
Ma, Long-Fei
Wen, Qiao-Qiao
Ma, Qiu-Juan
coredata.dc:description: In this work, water dispersible green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) as silicon source and amidol as reducing agent. The SiNPs features outstanding photobleaching resistance, salt resistance, water dispersion, and biocompatibility. The fluorescence of SiNPs could be distinctly quenched by curcumin via internal filtration effect (IFE) and electrostatic interaction. Based on this, a high sensitive fluorescent method for curcumin detection was established with a wide linear range of 0.05–50 µM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 15.2 nM. The developed method exhibited the advantages of high sensitivity, rapid response, low cost and simplicity. The method has been successfully applied to determine the content of curcumin in the actual samples of capsule health care products and curry powder. Additionally, owing to the negligible cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, SiNPs could be used for imaging with HeLa cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent silicon nanoparticles
High sensitive detection
Curcumin
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of curcumin in actual samples
Optimization of detection conditions for curcumin
Performance of SiNPs for curcumin detection
Possible mechanism of the optical response of SiNPs to curcumin
Analysis of curcumin in actual samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, CAS. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of SiNPs synthesis process (a) and SiNPs for fluorescent detection of curcumin (b).
(a) TEM images of SiNPs; (b) corresponding size distribution histogram; (c) XRD spectra of SiNPs; (d) FT-IR spectra of SiNPs.

High resolution XPS spectra of SiNPs. (a) Full-scan; (b) C 1s; (c) N 1s; (d) O 1s; (e) Si 2p spectra. (f) The fluorescence excitation (curve a), fluorescence emission (curve b), and UV-Vis absorption (curve c) spectra of SiNPs (λ ex = 444 nm, λ em = 500 nm).

(a) Normalized FL intensity of SiNPs at different pH values; (b) normalized FL intensity of SiNPs in NaCl solution of different concentrations; (c) FL intensity variation tendency of SiNPs at 444 nm of light illumination. The error bar is the standard deviation of three independent experiments.
(a) Linear relationship between normalized FL intensity of SiNPs and temperatures (5-85°C); (b) reversible response of FL intensity toward temperature change from 5°C to 85°C repeatedly.

(a) The fluorescence spectra of SiNPs after addition of different concentrations of curcumin. Inset: photographs of SiNPs in the absence (left) and presence (right) of curcumin under UV light (365 nm); (b) Log(F 0/F) of SiNPs as a function of the concentration of curcumin (F 0 and F are the FL intensities of SiNPs in the absence and presence of curcumin, respectively).

(a) FL intensity of SiNPs after addition of 200 μM interference ions (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 10 µM curcumin (red bars); (b) FL intensity of SiNPs after addition of 200 μM organic interfering substances (black bars) and the subsequent addition of 10 µM curcumin (red bars). The error bar is the standard deviation of three independent experiments.

(a) FL excitation spectra (curve a) and emission spectra (curve b) of SiNPs, UV-Vis absorption spectra of curcumin (curve c); (b) UV-Vis absorption spectra of SiNPs (SiNPs were diluted 20 times by PBS solution (10 mM, pH 7.4)), curcumin (10 µM), and the theoretical and experimental spectra of the mixture of SiNPs and curcumin.
Fluorescence images of HeLa cells incubated with SiNPs (100 µg/mL) at 37°C for 3 h. (a) Dark filed image; (b) bright filed image; (c) merged image of bright and dark filed image.
Comparison of methods for detecting curcumin.

row.entry: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of curcumin in real sample.

head.ce:title: Water dispersible green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles for high sensitive detection of curcumin and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, Zhengzhou 450046, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine, Zhengzhou, 450046, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Pharmacy, Henan University of Chinese Medicine

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, water dispersible green fluorescent silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method using 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) as silicon source and amidol as reducing agent. The SiNPs features outstanding photobleaching resistance, salt resistance, water dispersion, and biocompatibility. The fluorescence of SiNPs could be distinctly quenched by curcumin via internal filtration effect (IFE) and electrostatic interaction. Based on this, a high sensitive fluorescent method for curcumin detection was established with a wide linear range of 0.05–50 µM and a limit of detection (LOD) as low as 15.2 nM. The developed method exhibited the advantages of high sensitivity, rapid response, low cost and simplicity. The method has been successfully applied to determine the content of curcumin in the actual samples of capsule health care products and curry powder. Additionally, owing to the negligible cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, SiNPs could be used for imaging with HeLa cells.

Green fluorescent SiNPs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane as silicon source and amidol as reducing agent. A high sensitive fluorescent method based on the prepared SiNPs was established for curcumin detection via internal filtration effect and electrostatic interaction. The proposed method exhibited the advantages of high sensitivity, rapid response, low cost and simplicity.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin, the main active ingredient of turmeric, is widely used in medicine and health care products. Modern medicine showed that curcumin has a variety of pharmacological effects, such as lowering blood glucose [1,2], anti-inflammatory [3], anti-oxidation [4], and anti-cancer [5,6], with low toxicity and small adverse reactions. Nevertheless, overuse of curcumin could deactivate the body's DNA, resulting in decrease in intracellular ATP level, and causing irreversible effects such as tissue necrosis [7]. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a sensitive and simple method for curcumin detection. At present, the commonly analysis methods of curcumin were high-performance liquid chromatography [8–10], electrochemical analysis method [11–14], thin-layer chromatography [15], ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV-Vis) spectrophotometric method [16] and fluorescence method, etc. Among them, fluorescent method was widely used because of their merits of simple operation, low cost, and high selectivity and sensitivity. So far, various fluorescent nanomaterials including semiconductor quantum dots, graphene quantum dots, and carbon dots have been utilized as nano-probe for fluorescent detection of curcumin [17–22]. For example, Tade et al. prepared blue luminescent graphene quantum dots by bamboo timber waste derived cellulose nanocrystals and applied them for fluorescent curcumin detection [20]. Tang et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped fluorescence carbon dots for iodide and curcumin in biological and food samples [21]. Hao et al. synthesized carbon nanodots codoped with N, S, P, and Cl elements as “off-on” fluorescent probes for curcumin and Eu3+ detection [22]. Even though the above fluorescence probes could provide effective detection of curcumin, most of them exhibited complex preparation process of nano-probe, narrow detection ranges, and relatively low sensitivity. Developing a novel fluorescence probe with simple fabrication process and low toxicity for high sensitive detection of curcumin was still highly desirable.

Water dispersible silicon nanoparticles (SiNPs) are a kind of environmental-friendly fluorescent probes. It has been widely concerned for its excellent optical stability, low toxicity, good water solubility, and biocompatibility in recent years. At present, the synthesis methods of water dispersible SiNPs include post synthesis method [23,24], room temperature stirring method [25,26], microwave-assisted method [27–29], and hydrothermal method [30–33], etc. Among them, one-pot hydrothermal method was widely used because of its simple operation, low cost, relatively mild reaction conditions, and high quantum yield of synthesized materials. Synthetic SiNPs were widely used in the fields of detection and biological imaging in recent years. So far, a few fluorescence sensors based on SiNPs have been developed for the detection of curcumin. For example, Li et al. prepared hydrophilic blue fluorescent SiNPs as a turn-off fluorescent probe for curcuminoids detection with a limit of detection (LOD) of 17.6 ng/mL [34]. Our group previously developed water-dispersible silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) to detect curcumin with a detection limit of 91 nM [35]. Although few fluorescent methods have been developed to detect curcumin based SiNPs, it is rewarding to synthesize a novel fluorescent SiNPs with outstanding properties and develop a high sensitive method for simple curcumin detection.

In this work, by using 3-[2-(2-aminoethylamino)ethylamino]propyl-trimethoxysilane (AEEA) as silicon source and amidol as reducing agent, novel green fluorescent SiNPs was easily synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 a). The method requires no further modification and avoids tedious multi-step preparation process. The prepared SiNPs showed good photobleaching resistance, salt resistance and water dispersion. With the increasing concentration of curcumin, the fluorescence intensity (FL intensity) of SiNPs decreased significantly. Based on this, a high sensitive fluorescent detection method for quantitative determination of curcumin was established (Scheme 1b). This method exhibited the advantages of simple operation, low LOD, wide linear range, and rapid response. The method has been successfully applied to determine the content of curcumin in capsule health care products and curry powder. In addition, owing to the low toxicity and good biocompatibility, the prepared SiNPs could also be used as fluorescent reagents for cell imaging.

Firstly, 1 mL AEEA was added into 4 mL deionized water under 60°C water bath stirring. Then, 750 µL HCl (1 M) solution and 2.0 mg amidol was added to the above solution with continuous stirring for 10 min. After that, the mixture solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave for reaction at 200°C for 6 h. After reaction, the SiNPs solution was cooled to room temperature and then purified by dialysis bag (1000 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 7 h. The purified SiNPs solution was stored at 4°C for further characterization and detection of curcumin. A certain amount of purified SiNPs solution was lyophilized to produce SiNPs solid powder for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transformed infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) characterizations and cell experiments. After lyophilization, the synthesis yield of SiNPs was calculated to be 20.66 mg/mL.

Curcumin was dissolved in ethanol to prepare a stock solution with a concentration of 5 mM. Then, 100 µL SiNPs solution was added into 5.0 mL centrifuge tube followed by different volumes of curcumin solution. The above solution was diluted to 2.0 mL with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution (10 mM, pH 7.4). After 1 min incubation, the fluorescence emission spectra of SiNPs were measured at the excitation wavelength of 444 nm. The Log(F 0/F) value of SiNPs and the concentration of curcumin (F 0 and F are the FL intensity of SiNPs in the absence and presence of curcumin, respectively) were used to draw the working curve.

The selectivity of SiNPs to curcumin was evaluated by adding potential interferences including inorganic ions, amino acids, vitamins, maltose, glucose, and urea instead of curcumin. The anti-interference experiment was also performed by adding curcumin and interfering substances simultaneously under the same conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of curcumin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The 0.1 g capsule contents from two manufactures were weighed and dissolved in 10 mL of methanol, respectively. The 0.5 g of curry powder was weighed and dissolved in 7 mL of deionized water. After ultrasonic treatment for 30 min, the solution was centrifuged at 16500 r/min for 30 min. The supernatant was taken and diluted to an appropriate concentration for quantitative detection. The contents of curcumin in the above samples were detected by the fluorescence working curve. Known amount of curcumin was spiked into the samples for recovery experiment.

HeLa cells were cultured in completed dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin streptomycin. Standard MTT assay was used to detect the cytotoxicity of SiNPs to HeLa cells. Firstly, 100 µL HeLa cells suspension per well were seeded in a 96-well plate and placed in an incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) for 24 h. Then, the supernatant was discarded and fresh completed DMEM containing different concentrations of SiNPs (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, and 300 µg/mL) were added into the drug administration wells incubate for 3 h. Afterwards, the drug was discarded and 100 µL fresh completed DMEM was added into the wells. After incubation for 24 h, 10 µL MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added and the culture was continued for 4 h. Finally, the supernatant was discarded, 150 µL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well, and the optical density value was measured at 490 nm with a microplate reader to calculate the cell viability.

HeLa cells suspension was inoculated in a laser confocal culture dishes and placed in incubator (37°C, 5% CO2) for adherent culture. After 24 h, the supernatant was discarded and washed with dulbecco's phosphate buffered saline (DPBS) solution for three times, and fresh completed DMEM containing SiNPs (100 µg/mL) was added into laser confocal culture dishes. After 3 h of continuous culture, the supernatant was discarded and washed with DPBS solution for three times, and 2 mL DPBS solution was added to the laser confocal culture dishes for imaging.

In order to obtain SiNPs with excellent fluorescence properties, parameters such as the amount of reducing agent amidol, reaction temperature, reaction time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S1a, the FL intensity slightly decreased with the increasing amount of amidol. The result in Fig. S1b indicated obviously increase of the FL intensity by raising the temperature from 120°C to 200°C. As displayed in Fig. S1c, the FL intensity gradually increased for 6 h and then changed slightly. In conclusion, the highest FL intensity of SiNPs was obtained when the dosage of amidol was 2 mg, the reaction temperature was 200°C, and the reaction time was 6 h. In the control experiment, the fluorescence spectra of individual AEEA, amidol and the mixture of AEEA and amidol reacted under the same conditions were measured. As illustrated in Fig. S2, AEEA or amidol alone produced very weak fluorescence. Only when AEEA and amidol co-existed, obvious FL intensity could be observed, indicating that the fluorescence mainly derived from the products of AEEA and amidol.

The morphology and size of SiNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As seen in Fig. 1 a, the obtained SiNPs have regular shape and uniform distribution. The corresponding size distribution histogram (Fig. 1b) indicated that the particle size of SiNPs mainly ranged from 2.0 to 3.6 nm, with an average diameter of 2.89 nm. XRD results in Fig. 1c showed that SiNPs was in amorphous state [36]. The surface functional groups of SiNPs were studied by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1d). The absorption peak at 3417 cm−1 was assigned to O–H stretching vibration [37] . The absorption peaks at 2876 cm−1 and 2928 cm−1 demonstrated saturated C–H and unsaturated C–H stretching vibration, respectively [38]. The signal at 1584 cm−1 was attributed to C–N bending vibration [39]. The absorption peak at 1470 cm−1 was assigned to C=C skeleton vibration on aromatic hydrocarbon [17]. The absorption peak at 1313 cm−1 was attributed to C–O stretching vibration [17]. The absorption peak at 1025 cm−1 was ascribed the stretching vibration of Si–C [40], while the absorption peak at 1130 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of Si–O [41]. Two peaks at 676 cm−1 and 763 cm−1 belonged to the N–H secondary amine vibration and swing vibration [41]. The above results indicated that the surface of SiNPs is rich in hydrophilic amino and hydroxyl groups, which could improve the hydrophilicity and water stability of SiNPs. XPS experiment was also conducted to evaluate the structures and functional groups of SiNPs. In the whole scanning XPS spectrum, the peaks at 101.6 eV , 152.5 eV, 284.5 eV, 397.6 eV, and 531.6 eV (Fig. 2 a) belonged to Si 2p, Si 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. The XPS spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b) showed four peaks at 283.8 eV (C–Si), 284.6 eV (C–C/C=C), 285.5 eV (C–N), and 286.8 eV (C–OH/C–O–C) [42]. Four fitting peaks at 398.0 eV (N–Si), 398.3 eV (C–N–C), 398.7 eV (N–H), and 400.0 eV (N–(C)3) were observed in the XPS spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2c) [43]. In the XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 2d), three peaks were observed, of which two peaks at 530.9 eV and 531.2 eV belonged to Si-O, and the peak at 532.5 eV belonged to C–O [40]. The XPS spectrum of Si 2p (Fig. 2e) showed three peaks centered at 101.3 eV, 101.8 eV, and 102.4 eV, which were corresponded to Si–C, Si–N and Si–O, respectively [44]. The functional groups and surface composition of SiNPs conducted by XPS were consistent with FT-IR results. The fluorescence spectrum and UV-Vis spectrum of SiNPs were recorded. As illustrated in Fig. 2f, when the excitation wavelength was 444 nm (curve a), the fluorescence emission peak of SiNPs centered at 500 nm (curve b). The absorption peak (curve c) at 363 nm and 426 nm were assigned as π-π* transition of C=C [40]. The solution of SiNPs showed bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (insert in Fig. 2f). Absolute quantum yield of the prepared SiNPs was calculated to be 13.8%. Fig. S3 was the fluorescence emission spectra of SiNPs under different excitation wavelengths. The position of the fluorescence emission peak was basically unchanged with the change of excitation wavelength, indicating a size-independent emission behavior [33].

In order to investigate the stability of SiNPs, the fluorescence of SiNPs under different pH value, various concentrations of NaCl solution, illumination time, and different temperature conditions were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the FL intensity of SiNPs was strong and stable at pH 4.0–12.0, indicating good pH stability. As shown in Fig. 3b, even when the concentration of NaCl solution increased as high as 100 mM, the FL intensity of SiNPs was also very stable, showing good salt tolerance. Furthermore, as seen in Fig. 3c, the FL intensity of SiNPs remained stable after irradiation at 444 nm for 60 min, illustrating that SiNPs exhibited excellent photobleaching resistance.

Interestingly, the FL intensity of SiNPs is sensitive to temperature. As seen in Fig. 4 a, when the temperature increased from 5°C to 85°C, the FL intensity of SiNPs gradually decreased. A significant linear relationship between temperature and FL intensity could be observed in the range of 5–85°C. The main reason for the temperature sensing was that the molecular collision frequency and nonradiative transition rate increased as the temperature increase, while the radiation transition rate remained constant, resulting in the decrease in emission intensity of the excited state (i.e., the FL intensity) [45,46]. Furthermore, the reversible response of FL intensity at different temperature of 5°C and 85°C was shown in cyclic fashion. As shown in Fig. 4b, the FL intensity at the two temperatures was barely changed after four cycles, illustrating the sensitivity and stability of SiNPs. Therefore, SiNPs could be used as a potential fluorescent temperature probe for temperature measurement.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions for curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: To gain the highest sensitivity of SiNPs to curcumin, the pH value of PBS solution and the response time of SiNPs to curcumin were studied. As previously mentioned, the FL intensity of SiNPs remained almost constant at pH 4.0–12.0. Moreover, when the pH of PBS solution was 7.4, curcumin exhibited highest fluorescence quenching efficiency for SiNPs (Fig. S4a). Therefore, 7.4 was selected as the optimal pH value in the following experiments. As shown in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence quenching was very fast, and achieved stable within 30 s after mixing of SiNPs and curcumin solution. Hence, 1 min was selected as the optimal reaction time for the convenience of time control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of SiNPs for curcumin detection
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of the sensing system was evaluated under the optimal detection conditions. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the FL intensity of SiNPs decreased gradually with the increase of curcumin concentration. There was a good linear relationship between the quenching efficiency Log(F 0/F) and curcumin concentration in the range of 0.05–50 µM (Fig. 5b). LOD was 15.2 nM (3s/k, where s is the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve). The result indicated that SiNPs could be used as a high sensitive fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of curcumin. Compared with some previous literatures of curcumin determination (Table 1 ) [10,13,14,16-20,35], this method showed high sensitivity, wide linear range, low LOD, simple operation, and rapid response.

In order to evaluate the response selectivity of SiNPs to curcumin, 13 kinds of common inorganic ions, 11 kinds of amino acids, vitamin B12, vitamin C, maltose, glucose, and urea were selected as potential interference. As shown in Fig. 6 , the quenching efficiency of FL intensity of SiNPs could be ignored when these interferences were added into SiNPs. Afterwards, the anti-interference ability of SiNPs was also studied. When the mixture of curcumin and interference compound was added into the detection system, it was found that the FL intensity did not change significantly compared with the system only with curcumin. These results indicated that SiNPs possess outstanding selectivity and anti-interference ability for the detection of curcumin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the optical response of SiNPs to curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: The possible detection mechanism of SiNPs to curcumin was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 a, curcumin possessed a characteristic absorption peak at 429 nm. The UV-Vis absorption spectra of curcumin and the fluorescence excitation spectra of SiNPs were almost completely overlapped. In addition, the fluorescence lifetimes of SiNPs were estimated in the absence and presence of curcumin. The result in Fig. S5 and Table S1 showed that the change of fluorescence lifetime was negligible after addition of different concentrations of curcumin. Therefore, internal filtration effect (IFE) was considered to be the mechanism responsible for fluorescence quenching [47]. The potential interfering substances in the practical samples were unable to generate the absorbance peak at 429 nm, indicating that the method possessed high selectivity for curcumin detection. Furthermore, UV-Vis spectra of SiNPs in the absence or presence of curcumin were studied. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the experimental absorption spectra of the mixture of SiNPs and curcumin were not coincident with the theoretical absorption spectra, indicating that there was interaction between SiNPs and curcumin. According to the structures of SiNPs and curcumin, electrostatic interaction might exist between them. In order to confirm this, Zeta potential experiment was carried out. As seen in Fig. S6, the Zeta potentials of SiNPs solution, curcumin solution and the mixture of SiNPs and curcumin solution were 1.20 mV, −1.61 mV, and 0.56 mV, respectively. This phenomenon indicated that there was an electrostatic interaction between curcumin and SiNPs [48], which reduced the Zeta potential of SiNPs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SiNPs to curcumin was the synergy effects of IFE and electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of curcumin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the application of SiNPs, the content of curcumin in two capsule health care products and curry powder were detected by the established method. As seen in Table 2 , the contents of curcumin in the diluted extractions of three samples were 12.35 μM, 12.28 μM, and 11.17 μM, respectively. The recoveries of the established method were also investigated by adding a series of known concentrations of curcumin. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries ranged from 95.83% to 105.70%, and the relative standard deviations were less than 5.83% (n = 3), indicating the practicability of the method.

With the development of modern medical technology, increasing technologies are required to monitor the pathological changes of cells and tissues in the biological system. Fluorescence imaging based on nanotechnology shows great advantages in clinical medicine in recent years. Benefiting from their excellent optical stability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, SiNPs have been considered to be ideal fluorescent materials for bioimaging. In the following experiment, the cell imaging ability of the prepared SiNPs was preliminarily investigated in HeLa cells. To investigate the cytotoxicity of SiNPs, the MTT experiment was performed. The results in Fig. S7 showed that the survival rate of HeLa cells decreased slightly with the increase of SiNPs concentration. When the concentration of SiNPs was up to 300 µg/mL, the survival rate was still up to 90%, indicating that SiNPs had low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, and great potential for cell imaging. Fig. 8 showed the fluorescence image of SiNPs-labeled HeLa cells. After incubating the HeLa cells with SiNPs (100 μg/mL) for 3 h, the cells labeled with SiNPs were in good condition and a significant green emission could be observed from the intracellular region. The results indicated that SiNPs exhibited great potential for bioimaging applications.

In summary, green fluorescent water-soluble SiNPs with absolute quantum yield of 13.8% was synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The SiNPs exhibited excellent salt resistance, photobleaching resistance and water dispersion. Based on IFE and electrostatic interaction, SiNPs could be used as a high sensitive fluorescent probe for quantitative determination of curcumin with low LOD of 15.2 nM. The probe has been successfully applied to determine the content of curcumin in two capsule health care products and curry powder with a satisfactory recovery (95.83%–105.70%). In addition, because of its low cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility, SiNPs could be used for cell imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the Zhongjing Scholars Research Funding of Henan University of Chinese Medicine (00104311-2021-1-12).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2022.100111.
